nativeRange.habitat: Echinopora is found on shallow tropical reef slopes, reef flats, and lagoons across the Indo-Pacific and Red Sea, typically growing as flat plates spreading outward across exposed hard substrate. In favorable shallow zones with flat substrate and moderate light and water movement, some species — particularly E. lamellosa — can form locally dominant stands that spread across large sections of reef.

decorPreferences: stable rockwork base for encrusting or plating growth
lower to mid rockwork placement with indirect or moderate light
ample open space around the colony (8–12 cm clearance from neighbors)
moderate indirect flow, avoid direct laminar blast on tissue

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Supplemental target feeding once or twice weekly with small meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, Oyster-Feast, Roti-Feast, cyclopeeze, or baby brine shrimp is generally beneficial for growth and coloration. Feeding tends to occur at night when sweeper tentacles are extended; a turkey baster or pipette can be used for targeted delivery.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS corals placed well out of sweeper reach
avoidWith: corals within sweeper tentacle range (8–10 cm)
other Echinopora colonies without adequate spacing
aggressive LPS such as Galaxea or Hammer placed nearby
high-flow powerheads directed at colony surface

imageCandidates.notes: Donald Davesne; ~2048x1536. Echinopora lamellosa: striking lavender-purple colony in classic whorled/tiered plating growth form with dotted corallites, sharp, whole colony in-situ. Best hero for the genus.
Bernard DUPONT; 4416x2711. Echinopora sp. (Edgehog/Hedgehog Coral): large, tack-sharp flat plate showing the diagnostic raised spiny corallites of the genus in-situ.
Julien Bidet for MDC Seamarc; 4000x3000. Echinopora horrida: sharp in-situ shot of the branching/nodular growth form, warm yellow colouration, whole colony framed.
Ryan McMinds / Joe Pollock; 2867x2393. Echinopora mammiformis: vibrant orange branching colony with lavender-tipped polyps, sharp, whole colony in-situ.
Lyu yi-chi; ~2048x1536. Echinopora gemmacea: sharp in-situ plating colony with pale margins and honeycomb corallites, another distinct species for the genus spread.
Michelle Jonker; 2212x1408. Echinopora pacificus: plating colony with prominent corallites, adds a fifth distinct species; sharp, whole colony.

Laszlo; 4032x3024. Echinopora lamellosa under actinic/blue reef lighting showing neon green fluorescent polyps on a blue base - the hobbyist look; kept as a non-recommended extra because it is a tight macro crop, not a whole colony.

summary: The Hedgehog Coral (Echinopora sp.) is a plating and encrusting large polyp stony (LPS) coral from the Indo-Pacific, generally considered intermediate in care. It prefers moderate light and flow and needs ample open space around the colony, as it extends stinging sweeper tentacles at night that can damage neighboring corals.
careNotes:
Chalice Coral trade name: In the aquarium trade, 'Chalice Coral' is a broad commercial umbrella name applied to several unrelated genera. The core chalice group consists of Echinophyllia (see [echinophyllia-coral]) and Oxypora (see [oxypora-coral]), both in family Lobophylliidae. Echinopora and Mycedium (see [mycedium-coral]) belong to a different family, Merulinidae, but are sold under the same trade name because of a similar flat, plating growth form — convergent appearance rather than close relationship.

Taxonomy: Echinopora (Lamarck, 1816) belongs to family Merulinidae, order Scleractinia. Older sources may list the family as Faviidae, which was the accepted placement before molecular studies prompted a reclassification around 2012; Merulinidae is the current accepted family. The genus comprises approximately 13 recognized species, including E. lamellosa (the most commonly encountered species in both trade and scientific literature), E. gemmacea, E. horrida, E. fruticulosa, and E. mammiformis. Most specimens sold in the aquarium trade are offered at the genus level as 'Echinopora sp.' or by a color-morph trade name without species-level identification.

Growth form: Echinopora typically grows as thin flat plates, encrusting sheets, or whorled, tiered structures spreading outward across hard substrate. Wild colonies can exceed 5 meters across; captive colonies generally reach 10–40 cm. Growth tends to be relatively fast compared to many other large polyp stony corals, with encrusting expansion visible over weeks under good conditions.

Aquacultured strains: ORA has produced several aquacultured Echinopora strains, including the well-known 'Sprung's Stunner' (E. lamellosa with a purple base and bright green polyps) and other named color morphs sold under trade names such as 'Firefly Echinopora' and 'Blue Chalice Echinopora.'

Lighting: Echinopora tends to adapt to a wider light range than many chalice-family corals, but very high PAR — above around 250 — can cause bleaching. New specimens should be acclimated gradually over 2–3 weeks, starting at the lower end of the target light range before moving to the final placement.

Flow: Moderate flow is recommended — enough to oxygenate the tissue and clear debris from around the colony, but not so strong that it causes sustained polyp retraction. Avoid pointing a powerhead directly at the colony surface.

Aggression: Echinopora is generally considered semi-aggressive. It extends sweeper tentacles at night — reported to reach 5–10 cm in length — that can sting and damage neighboring corals. Placement should account for the full reach of nighttime extension, and a clearance of at least 8–12 cm in all directions from neighboring colonies is advisable.

Water quality: Stable parameters are important for long-term health. Nitrates in the range of 2–10 ppm and phosphates around 0.03–0.1 ppm are generally reported as workable; very low-nutrient systems may cause color loss. Calcium, alkalinity, and magnesium should be supplemented as for other large polyp stony corals.

Care level: The intermediate designation reflects a range within the genus — E. lamellosa and most ORA aquacultured strains are among the more forgiving large polyp stony corals, while wild-collected specimens of E. horrida can be more sensitive and demanding.

Fragging: Colonies can be fragged with bone cutters or a tile saw. Frags of 2.5 cm or larger encrust and recover well on frag plugs or rubble secured with coral glue. Dipping new frags in a coral dip solution before introduction is advisable.
breedingNotes: Fragging is the primary propagation method in captivity. ORA has successfully aquacultured multiple Echinopora strains, and captive-grown frags are available through specialty online coral retailers and some local fish stores that stock reef corals. Sexual broadcast spawning occurs on wild reefs but is not practiced in home aquaria.

primary.name: Vivid Aquariums – Firefly Echinopora Coral
primary.notes: Primary source for husbandry parameters: PAR 80–250, temperature 75–80°F, salinity 1.024–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, alkalinity 8–9.5 dKH, calcium 420–440 ppm, magnesium 1260–1350 ppm, feeding recommendations. Used as primary after WWC (404) and BRS care article (404) both failed.

additional.name: Reeflings – Hedgehog Coral (Echinopora lamellosa) Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (24–27°C), salinity (1.023–1.025), pH (8.1–8.4), lighting (150–250 PAR), flow (moderate), aggression, and care level. Also confirmed common names (Hedgehog Coral, Honeycomb Coral, Spiny Plate Coral, Blue Chalice). Minor disagreement on PAR upper bound (250 vs 200) resolved by averaging across all sources.

additional.name: Reeflex – Echinopora lamellosa
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (24.7–28.9°C), minimum tank size (150L/33 gal), adult captive size (up to 25 cm), native range (Indo-Pacific, depth 3–35 m), sweeper tentacle length (up to 10 cm), flow (moderate), feeding (phyto/zooplankton). Family listed as Merulinidae, order Scleractinia confirmed.

additional.name: ReefChasers – Chalice Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Confirmed care level (intermediate), flow (moderate indirect), PAR range (75–100 for chalice genus), alkalinity (8–9 dKH), calcium (430–460 ppm). Confirmed Echinopora as one of 8 chalice-umbrella genera. Minor disagreement on calcium upper bound (460 vs 450) resolved by averaging.

additional.name: Corals of the World – Echinopora lamellosa
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (genus Echinopora Lamarck 1816), colony growth form description (thin laminae in whorls/tiers), wild colony size documentation (stands exceeding 5 m across), and habitat note (dominant in shallow flat-substrate habitats). Not used for husbandry parameters.

additional.name: Reef Builders – ORA Sprung's Stunner Echinopora lamellosa
additional.notes: Used for ORA aquaculture history and trade name documentation (Sprung's Stunner — purple base, green polyps). Confirmed hardy LPS character, fast growth, sweeper tentacle caution at night.
